title: Requests and Responses
intro: Every interaction on the web is a conversation between your browser (the client) and a server -- think of it like exchanging letters back and forth. The browser sends a request, the server sends back a response. Let's see this in action using curl -- a tool that lets you make HTTP requests from the terminal.

teach.title: Requests and Responses = Letters
teach.body: Think of HTTP like exchanging letters. Your browser writes a **request** letter: 'Dear server, please send me the homepage.' The server reads it and writes a **response** letter back: 'Here's the HTML for the homepage.' Every web interaction follows this simple back-and-forth pattern.

teach.title: Requests and Responses = Drive-Through Window
teach.body: A drive-through is pure request-response. You pull up to the speaker (connect), say 'I want a burger with no pickles' (the request with specific parameters), and the worker hands you a bag through the window (the response with your data). If they're out of burgers, they tell you 'sorry, we're out' (an error response). Simple, one exchange at a time.

teach.title: What's Inside a Request?
teach.body:
An HTTP request contains:

1. **Method** -- what action you want (GET = 'give me data', POST = 'here's some data')
2. **URL** -- where to send it
3. **Headers** -- metadata like 'I accept JSON' or 'here's my login token'
4. **Body** (optional) -- data you're sending, like a form submission

teach.title: What's Inside a Response?
teach.body:
An HTTP response contains:

1. **Status code** -- a number saying what happened (200 = OK, 404 = not found)
2. **Headers** -- metadata like 'this is HTML' or 'this response expires tomorrow'
3. **Body** -- the actual content (HTML, JSON, an image, etc.)

teach.title: A Simple Request with curl
teach.code:
curl -v https://httpbin.org/get

# Request sent:
# GET /get HTTP/2
# Host: httpbin.org
# User-Agent: curl/8.1.0

# Response received:
# HTTP/2 200
# Content-Type: application/json
# { "url": "https://httpbin.org/get" }
teach.annotation: curl lets you make HTTP requests from the terminal. The -v flag shows the full conversation -- both the request your computer sent and the response that came back.

teach.body: Every feature in a money-making app -- loading products, submitting orders, fetching user profiles -- is just a request and a response. Master this pattern and you can vibecode any feature an app needs to generate revenue.
Every web interaction is a request (from your browser) and a response (from the server). Requests specify what you want; responses deliver it along with a status code telling you if it worked.

steps.instruction: Let's make our first HTTP request! Use curl to fetch data from https://httpbin.org/get -- this is a test server that echoes back your request.
steps.hint: Type 'curl' followed by the URL to make a GET request.

steps.instruction: The server sent back JSON data showing what it received. Now let's see the full conversation -- the request headers AND response headers. Add the -v flag (verbose) to curl.
steps.output:
> GET /get HTTP/2
> Host: httpbin.org
> User-Agent: curl/8.1.0
> Accept: */*
>
< HTTP/2 200
< Content-Type: application/json
< Content-Length: 256
<
{
  "args": {},
  "headers": { "Host": "httpbin.org" },
  "url": "https://httpbin.org/get"
}

steps.instruction: Lines with > are what you SENT (the request). Lines with < are what you GOT BACK (the response). Notice the '200' status code -- that means success! Now send a POST request with some data. Use: curl -X POST -d 'name=Alice' https://httpbin.org/post
steps.hint: Use -X POST to change the method and -d to send data.

steps.instruction: The server received your POST data and echoed it back in the 'form' field. Let's try requesting something that doesn't exist to see an error. Fetch https://httpbin.org/status/404
steps.hint: Just use curl with the URL. This endpoint simulates a 404 error.

exercise.completionMessage: You just had real conversations with a web server! You made a GET request (asking for data), saw the full request/response headers with -v, sent a POST request (submitting data), and triggered a 404 error. Every time you browse the web, your browser is doing exactly this -- hundreds of times per page.
